text: DANUBE (CIRCLE ON THIS SIDE OF THE), one of the four great divisions of Hungary Proper, comprising a considerable portion of the central districts of the kingdom, to the NE and E of the Danube, and embracing the coms. of Arva, Bács, Bárs, Gran, Pest, Neograd, Sohl, Honth, Neutra, Presburg, Trentsin, Turocs, and Lipta. It has an area of 953 Austrian sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 3,139,100.
DANVERS, a township in Essex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 16 m. N of Boston. Pop. 5,020.

DANVILLE, a township in Cumberland co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 42 m. SW of Augusta, skirted by the Androscoggin on the NE. Pop. 1,294. -Also a township in Rockingham co., in New Hampshire, 30 m. SE of Concord, intersected by Achusnut river. Pop. 538.—Also the cap. of Caledonia co., in the state of Vermont, 30 m. NE of Montpelier. Pop. of t. 2,633.—Also the cap. of Columbia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 71 m. N by E of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,000.—Also a v. in Pittsylvania co., in the state of Virginia, 164 m. SW by W of Richmond, on Dan river, an affluent of the Roanoke. Pop. 1,200. -Also a v. in Union township, in Knox co., in the state of Ohio, 65 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 400.— Also the cap. of Boyle co., in Kentucky, 41 m. S by W of Frankfort. Pop. 1,223. It is the seat of Centre college.—Also a v. in Sumter co., in Georgia, 100 m. SW of Milledgeville, on the W bank of the Flint. -Also the cap. of Hendricks co., in Iowa, 20 m. W of Indianapolis.—Also the cap. of Vermillion co., in Illinois, 130 m. E by N of Springfield. Pop. 600.— Also the cap. of Montgomery co., in Missouri, 47 m. N by E of Jefferson. Pop. 1,299,
DANZE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Morée, 6 m. NNW of Vendome. Pop. 1,094.
DANZIG. See DANTZIC.
DAO, or Douw, an island in the Asiatic archipelago, 55 m. SSW of the SW point of Timor, S lat. 11° 7', E long. 122° 40'.
DAODPOTRA. See BHAWALPUR.
DAON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Bierné, 5 m. S of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 942.
DAOUD, or Dowad, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwah, 50 m. NE of Champanir.
DAOUDCANDY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the E side of the Megna, below the junction of the Gomut or Gumti, 30 m. W of Comillah.
DAOUDNAGUR. See DAUDNAGAR.
DAOUD-PASHA, a village of Turkey, 4 m. W of district and 27 m. SW of Novo-Troki.

DAOULAS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, arrond. of Brest.-The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1841, 16,412.—The com. and v., 10 m. E of Brest, on the coast, had a pop. of 459.
DAOULETABAD. See DOWLETABAD.
DAOULGHAT. See DAWULGHAUT.

DAOURIAN MOUNTAINS, a range in Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, between the parallels of 50° and 53°. This range passes to S of the Onon, and along the Argun. It is sometimes called the Nertschinsk mountains. Another chain running NE, and separating the tributary streams of the Lena and Amur, receives the general designation of the Daourian mountains. A little to the W of the sources of the Olekma, this chain takes the name of the Jablannoi mountain, or 'the Mountain of apples,' on account of the rounded shape of the blocks composing it. Acquiring increased elevation as it approaches the Eastern ocean, it begins, near the sources of the Aldan, to bear the name of the Stannovoi mountains, which afterwards gives place to the vague denomination of the mountains of Okhotsk, which are said to reach Behring's straits.

DAPITAN, a village on the NE coast of Mindanao, in the Philippine group, at the embouchure of a small river. DAR, DARA, DIYAR, or DAIR, different forms of the same Arabic word, primitively signifying 'an abode,' house,' or 'fortress;' and of frequent occurrence in Eastern and Moorish topography, as a compound word in the names of localities.
DAR-EL-HAJAR. See BATN-EL-HAJAR.

DARABGERD, or DARABJERD, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Faristan, 132 m. SE of Shiraz. It was in former times a place of great importance and wealth, but its pop. is now reduced to about 15,000. The town is surrounded with groves of orange and lemon trees. Tobacco is extensively cultivated in the environs; and moum, a species of petroleum, is collected in considerable quantity in a cavern in the neighbourhood.

DARAGH, or GLENROE, a parish in co. Limerick, 6 m. SE of Kilmallock. Area 6,713 acres. Pop. 2,088. DARAH, DRA, or DRAHA, a province of Marocco, to the W of Tarudant, and E of Sigilmessa, extending from the Greater Atlas to the Sahara, and watered by a large river of the same name, which runs S, and appears to lose itself in the sands of the Desert.
DARAHA', a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which flows into the Rio Negro, on the l. bank, between the parishes of Lamalonga and Santa Izabel.

DARAJEE, a town of Sinde, on the Buggaur or W branch of the Indus, in N lat. 24° 30'. It is the port of Tatta. Pop. 2,000. The Buggaur contains 2 fath, water as high as D.; but shallows above that town. At high water there is everywhere a depth of 2 fath., and more frequently of from 5 to 6 fath., up to D.—Burnes.

DARAKOL, a village of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Diarbekir, in N lat. 38° 26', at an alt. of 2,993 ft. above sea-level. It is situated on the Sarum-Su, an affluent of the Tigris. Pop. about 300. The soil here is an arid white clay.
DARAMPOORY, a town of Hindostan, in the presidency of Madras, prov. and 35 m. N of Salem, and 120 m. W of Pondicherry.
DARANAGAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, district of Nuradabad, 74 m. NE of Delhi, in N lat. 29° 17'.
DARAOU, a considerable Fellah village on the E bank of the Nile, about 10 hours N of Assuan.
DARAPORAM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. DAR
DAOUGHI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Vilna, 1 of Coimbatur, near the Amaravati, in N lat. 10° 37'.
DARBACH, a river of Daghistan, in the Russian gov. of Caucasus, which, descending from the Kochmadaz, flows into the Caspian 10 m. N of Derbent, after a course of 50 m. from W to E.
DARBEIDA. See DAR-AL-BEIDA.

DARBIA, a region in the interior of Africa, the existence of which has been reported by the German missionary, Kugler, from information obtained by him during his residence in Egypt, from an individual belonging to D. Its people call themselves Magajine; and the country is about 300 m. SW from Dar-far. There are three considerable rivers in this country: the largest of these is the Lea or 'White river;' the second is called Karo or 'the River of reeds;' and the third is the Gililo or 'Black river.' The soil of D. is fertile; but nobody claims property in it. It sometimes snows in this country, but the snow melts as it falls to the ground. The Magajine are a free people, and governed by good and just laws. They have a headman or chief; but personal merit is the only ground of rank with them. They are active and industrious; but have few wants, and no coin or circulating medium. They have an idea of a Supreme Being, of the immortality of the soul, and the existence of guardian angels. They have a great annual festival, at which their whole tribe meet on the banks of a river with their great priest; the people bathe, and the priest marks the sign of the cross on the chest of each person with mud from the river. Besides the great priest, they have a sheikh or senior in every considerable hamlet, who prays for the sick when requested. Circumcision is in general use, though not imperative on any. Their language has many sounds in common with the Amharic.
DARBUNG, a river of Kunawur, rising near the Manerung pass [alt. 18,611 ft.], in N lat. 32° 56', E long. 78° 20', and flowing SSE to the Sampu.
DARBURRA, a fortress of Afghanistan, on the Daman, 12 m. NW of Tak, in N lat. 32° 18'.

DARBY, a township in Delaware co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 95 m. ESE of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,267.—Also a township in Pickaway co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,650.—Also a township in Madison co., in Ohio. Pop. 466.
DARBY (CAPE), a headland on the W coast of Russian America, in N lat 64° 12'.

DARBY CREEK, a small river of Ohio, U. S., rising in Union co., and flowing into the Scioto nearly opposite Circleville, after a course of 60 m.—Also a v. in Union co., in Ohio, 22 m. NNW of Columbus.
DARBY PLAINS, a village in Union co., in Ohio, U. S., 26 m. NNW of Columbus.
DARBYVILLE, a village in Pickaway co., in Ohio, U. S., 38 m. S of Columbus, on the W bank or Darby creek. Pop. 300.
DARCH'S ISLAND, a thickly wooded island to the E of Cape Croker, on the coast of Arnheim's- Land, in N Australia, in N lat. 11° 7½. It is 23 m. in length.
DARCHAU, or GANGARI, a town of Tibet, at the foot of Mount Gangdis, in N lat. 31° 4'. It forms an entrepot in the commercial intercourse between Lassa and Ladak.
DARDA, or Darda-Nagy, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Baranya, 4 m. N of Essek, on the l. bank of the Drave, in N lat. 45° 38'. Pop. 1,400.
DARDANELLE, a village in Yell co., in the state of Arkansas, U. S., 72 m. NW by W of Little Rock, on the SW of Arkansas river. Pop. 227.

DARDANELLES (THE), a strait which unites the Archipelago, or Ægean sea, with the sea of Marmora. Strictly speaking, the D. are the fortifications erected on both sides of this strait for its defence; but the name has at last attached itself to the strait which was classically known as the Hellespont. The view from the junction of the D. with the Ægean sea is interesting from its classical associations. In front are the isles of Imbros (I) and Samothrace, to the SE of which is Lemnos, with the distant summit of Athos on the coast of Macedonia. On the r. lies Tenedos (T), and the small isles which form a group opposed to Cape Jenicher, the ancient Sigæan promontory. The length of this strait is upwards of 50 m. in a direction NE true; but its width varies greatly. At its mouth towards the Archipelago, betwixt Seddul-Bahr, or the new castle of Europe (a), and Kum- Kal'-eh-si, or the new castle of Asia (b), it is 2 m. across with a depth of 34 fath. in the middle. About 5 m. above this it has a breadth of 5 m., but contracts between Killis-Bahr, or the old castle of Europe (c), and Saltanieh-Kal'-eh-si, or the old castle of Asia (d), 14 m. from the Archipelago, to less than a 1 m. At a point 31 m. above this, it is only 1 m. in breadth, with a depth, in mid-channel, of from 15 to 18 fath. Throughout the remaining part of the strait to the sea of Marmora, a distance of 32 m., the breadth varies from 2 to 3 m., and the depth, in midchannel, is from 26 to 42 fath. The town of the Dardanelle proper, called Charnak-Kal'-eh-si, is in the immediate vicinity of the old castle of Asia. It contains about 2,000 wooden houses, and has an extensive manufactory of pottery. Its inhabitants are chiefly Jews. It is the seat of all the consular establishments, and of the pasha of the D. The point of Abydus, and the opposite cliffs of Sestos, are about 42 m. above the old castles. On the supposed site of the ancient Abydus is a battery called Mackarah- Burnu, mounting 84 guns. The little cape of Abydus projects from the margin of an easy sloping plain, and contrasts finely with the bolder Sestos, a tall rugged cliff, a little up the strait, 11 m. from the opposite cape. Some grey and ivied ruins adorn the cliffs of Sestos, and a deep curve in the shore makes a beautiful cove. The last battery, on going up the strait, is one of 30 guns, called Chamli-Burnu, on the point of Sestos. From Sestos to Lampsaki, the 00 width of the strait is from 2 to 21 m. The lighthouse of the bay of Gallipoli (G) on the European side, 13 m. from the entrance of the sea of Marmora, and the usual anchorage of the Ottoman fleet, is in N. lat. 40° 24′ 30″, E long. 26° 39′ 45″. The strait opens beautifully into the expanse of the sea of Marmora, with the island of Marmora in the middle, and the shores on either side blue and hazy in the distance. A strong current, and which cannot easily be stemmed without a favourable S wind, flows at the rate of from 2 to 4 m. an hour through the strait, from the sea of Marmora to the Archipelago, and the Etesian wind blows in the same direction from May to September. Besides the natural difficulties of the navigation, there are great natural facilities on both shores for placing artillery in advantageous positions to defend the passage; and the Turks have erected tremendous fortifications on both sides for the purpose of defending the approach to their cap. from the side of the Mediterranean. Admiral Duckworth indeed boldly forced the strait in 1807, with 7 ships-of-the-line, and 2 frigates; and in returning, once more run the gauntlet of its monster guns, with the loss of 250 men killed or wounded; but since that time the fortifications have been greatly improved and increased in number. These works originally consisted of four castles,-two on the European and two on the Asiatic side. Those called the new castles, are gigantic white masses of fortification situated, as already mentioned, near the entrance of the strait, from the Archipelago, where it is little more than 2 m. across. The castle of Europe in this quarter, on Cape Greco, is provided with 70 guns and 4 mortars; that of Asia, on a sandy point, extending from Cape Jenicher or Janissary, has 80 guns and 4 mortars. About 12 m. further to the NE are the old castles. That in Europe, called Kellis-Bahar, is mounted with 50 guns; that of Asia with 84. It seems that the name Dardanelles is now especially applied to the different fortifications erected in modern times on both sides of the strait between these castles. In these new works, 64 guns are mounted in Europe, and 120 in Asia. The number of guns mounted in all the fortifications, and some others of less importance, is 689, besides 8 mortars. "Strong as the Dardanelles are," says Major Keppel, "where in every direction the eye rests upon guns bearing on a short and very narrow passage, the most dangerous part is the approach to the inner [old] castles. Their strength consists in the position of the castle of Sestos, to which, from the nature of the current, and from the banks running far out, a ship must come stern on and be inevitably raked without the power of returning more than her bowguns until just abreast, by which time she would be dreadfully cut up if the fire were at all well directed." [Journey across the Balcan.] The same authority states that the batteries are well-built, and placed in spots judiciously chosen; and that the greater number of the guns are solidly fixed on large blocks of wood, à fleur d'eau, and can consequently sweep its surface; but that they have no carriages, and cannot be pointed; so that they can effectively fire only when a ship covers them. Among them are several immense guns, of a calibre varying from 18 in. to 3 ft. in diam., from which huge stone shot are thrown. The quantity of powder which these large guns require is enormous: the largest requires to be charged with 330 lbs.; and as their muzzles project beyond the face of the works, and there is no parapet or breastwork to cover the gunners, they are kept ready to fire at any vessel coming within their line of action.-No ship-of-war is permitted to pass between these forts without a special permission from Constantinople. But the question has been more than ~ once agitated whether the straits are to be held as commencing at the New, or at the Old or Inner castles. On the 1st of November 1849 an English squadron of 12 ships-of-war having anchored in the bay between Punta-dei-Barbieri (g) and the inner castles, it was argued by Russian and Austrian diplomatists that this was a violation of the treaty of 1841. Yet this appears to be a station which, both by treaty and precedent, the English fleet was entitled to take up. In fact, both Russian and Austrian vessels have repeatedly entered the D. and anchored below the inner castles, before receiving their firman to pass the straits. On proceeding up the strait, the S or Asiatic coast, gradually rising from the sea to the range of Jela, presents a fine and apparently fertile but depopulated country. The European coast is more abrupt than that of Asia, but in several parts presents fruitful and well-cultivated valleys. The scenery on the banks of the strait of the D. is described by Macfarlane as being "by no means picturesque; it is a desert with, at long distances, some miserable hamlets devoid of wild or romantic features. One thing, however," adds this observer, "which I had seen described in poetry, struck me here with the force of reality-grass was growing within a span of the sea's water, and here and there dwarf trees seemed to have their roots beneath the waves. Bright verdant fields and declivities were separated from the blue of Marmora's wide basin but by a line of yellow sand." This is to be attributed to the comparative freshness of the waters of those seas flowing from the Euxine, which is fed by so many rivers; and the same cause probably accounts for the freezing of the Black sea.

DARDESHEIM, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, regency of Magdeburg, circle and 10 m. NW of Halberstadt, on a hill. Pop. in 1837, 1,555. It contains a church and an hospital, and in the environs are quarries of free-stone.
DARDILLY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Limonest, 4 m. NW of Lyon. Pop. 1,027.
DARENTH, or DARENT, a parish of Kent, 24 m. SSE of Dartford, on a river of the same name. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 698.
DARENTH (South), a hamlet partly in the parishes of Darenth and Horton-kirby. Pop. 195.
DARESBURG, a chapelry in the p. of Runcorn, Cheshire, 51 m. NE of Frodsham, near the Warrington and Newton railway, and on the Bridgewater canal. Area 530 acres. Pop. 184.

DARFIELD, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, ø 51 m. SE of Barnesley, on the Dearne, on the Leeds and Derby railway, and near the Dearne and Dove canal. Area 11,740 acres. Pop. 7,519. The town of D. is beautifully situated on the slope of a hill.

DARFO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 30 m. NE of Bergamo, district and 9 m. SSW of Breno, in the Val-Canonica, 7 m. NE from the lake of Iseo, and on the l. bank of the Oglio. Pop. 1,200. It has a considerable trade in silk, iron, and wood.

DAR-FOK, a territory of Africa, lying to the S of the district of Kamamyl, on the Toumat river, in Tropical Africa. It is described as a mountainous and thickly-wooded region, inhabited by savage tribes.

DAR-AL-BEIDA, or DARBEYDA, a village of Marocco, in the prov. and 160 m. WSW of Fez, and 56 m. SW of Salee, on a bay which affords good anchorage. In the vicinity are the ruins of Anofoe or Anafa, a town which was destroyed by the Portuguese in 1468.

DAR-FAYAL (WADY), a valley of North Africa, which the objects of trade are combined with those <sup>*</sup>スタングバングが、グラングググダブグ DAR in Tripoli, in the Jebel-Toubrouk, running along the coast, between the wadys Sedd and Dafney.
DAR-FUNGARA, a district of Sudan, in the S part of Dar-fur.

DAR-FUR, a small state in Tropical Africa; bounded on the E by Kordofan, by which it is separated from Abyssinia, and part of which it has recently subdued; on the N by the Desert, and on the NW by Bergu; part of the W frontier is bounded by Bergu, the rest of this frontier, and the whole of the S frontier, are bounded by various petty Negro kingdoms. If the map constructed from Browne's travels in Africa be correct, it is situated between 11° and 16° N lat. and between 26° and 29° 30' E long. Its length is consequently 345 m., and its breadth 230 m. This region was scarcely known to Europeans, even by name, when Browne visited it in 1793, and reached Cobbeh. The country rises towards the S; and a chain of mountains extends along the E frontier. The surface is highly diversified. -Situated among surrounding deserts, it has a soil of a similar character, which seems to be rendered capable of cultivation only by the existence of springs of water, and the periodical rains which are common in all tropical regions. This country has neither lake nor marsh; but water abounds in the southern districts, -which are watered by the Ada, a branch of the Bahr-el-Abiad. The rainy season commences with the middle of June, and terminates in September. If the quantity of rain be less than is necessary to bestow sufficient moisture for vegetation, the country is subjected to all the horrors of a famine. The rainwhich is generally heavy, and accompanied with lightning—is said to fall chiefly between three in the afternoon and midnight. The winds in D., unlike those in Egypt, are not periodical. The SE wind, as it blows from the nearest sea, brings the greatest quantity of rain. The N and NW winds are refreshing, but blow seldom. S winds bring the greatest heat. From the latter quarter proceed blasts which are said to fill the air with thick dust; and which seem to be similar to the simum of Egypt, and the harmattan of the W coasts of Africa. Browne observed pillars of sand, but they had not that tremendous appearance which Bruce ascribes to those which he saw in the neighbouring wilderness. Like the ancient kings of Egypt, and the modern kings of Bornu, the sovereign of D. annually evinces his regard for agriculture, by going out into the field and, with his own hands, performing the process of planting or sowing. The grain is sown at the commencement of the harif, or 'wet season,' and at the return of fair weather it is ripe; the ears are gathered by the women and slaves. After they are thrashed, the grain is dried in the sun, and laid up in holes made in the earth, lined with chaff. Millet, rice, maize, sesame, beans, and legumes are reared. The date-palm and tamarind are found here. Tobacco grows wild. The domestic animals are the camel, sheep, and goat.—D., from its situation, can only have an inland commerce; but this is extensive. Its grand intercourse is with Egypt, and is carried on by the system of caravans. The exports to Egypt consist chiefly of slaves taken in the Negro countries to the S, camels, ivory, the horns, teeth, and hides of the rhinoceros and hippopotamus, ostrich-feathers, gum, pimento, paroquets, and a small quantity of white copper. The imports are extremely various, comprising beads of all sorts, toys, glass, arms, cloth of different kinds, chiefly made in Egypt, with some of French manufacture, red Barbary caps, small carpets, silks wrought and unwrought, shoes, &c. There is also a considerable intercourse with Mecca, in of religion. Commerce is conducted entirely by barter; which greatly increases the difficulty of conducting it. In their persons, the Darfurians differ from the Negroes on the coast of Guinea, though their complexion is quite black, and their hair generally short and woolly. The Arabs, who are numerous, retain their distinction of features, colour, and language. The greater part of their food consists of grain, generally maize, pounded in a mortar, and formed sometimes into a pudding or into thin cakes. They are unwearied dancers, and each tribe seems to have its appropriate dance. At the time of Browne's visit, Islamism had not been established in this country more than 150 years. Their religion allows them four wives, a privilege of which all the D. who can afford it take advantage. The nobles have a greater number; and Teraub, one of their late kings, when he travelled, generally took, it is said, 500 along with him, besides leaving as many at home! These women are considered as a kind of servants; and are obliged to perform every species of domestic drudgery, and to labour in the fields. The ablutions prescribed by the Koran are not scrupulously performed, nor are they remarkable for personal cleanliness. In defiance also of the law of their prophet, a fermented liquor called merise—the same with the bouza of the Negroes-is universally indulged in by persons of all descriptions, and of both sexes. Browne, from whose statements almost all our knowledge of D. is derived, accuses the people of vices which in the greater part of countries are unknown. According to him, "every species of incest is so common as to meet with little blame. The vices of thieving, lying, and cheating in bargains, with all others nearly or remotely allied to them, are almost universal. No property, whether considerable or trifling, is safe out of the sight of the owner, nor indeed scarcely in it, unless he be stronger than the thief. In buying and selling, the parent glories in deceiving the son, and the son the parent; and God and the prophet are hourly invocated to give colour to the most palpable frauds and falsehoods." Browne found it difficult to form a very precise idea of the population; but as the army did not exceed 2,000 men, and was even considered as a large force, he did not conceive that the whole pop. could exceed 200,000. Arabic is almost as generally spoken as the language of the country, and lawsuits are conducted in both languages.—The government is despotic. The monarch cannot safely violate the Koran; but beyond it his will has no limits. The ministers of religion hold a rank next to the officers of state; they alone have a right to make remonstrances, but possess no power of enforcing them. A more formidable power resides with the army: no sovereign can safely incur the displeasure of that body. The royal revenue is derived from various sources: viz., a duty on all merchandise imported, amounting to about a tenth; fines for misdemeanours, and to which is added, in case of murder, a certain proportion of the property of the village in which it is committed; presents continually received from the great men, merchants, and all descriptions of persons; a tribute imposed on the neighbouring Arab tribes; and lastly, the king's profits as a merchant, exporting and importing a great quantity of goods, for which he has means of procuring a market not possessed by private individuals. Cobbeh, the cap., in N lat. 14° 11', E long. 28° 8', has not more than 6,000 inhabitants, who are almost all foreigners. They consist chiefly of merchants, and come partly from Egypt, partly from the eastern countries of Dongola, Sennaar, and Kordofan. NE by N from Cobbeh is Hellet-Hassan, inhabited altogether by the people of Dongola. The other princi- DAR pal towns are: Sweini, the frontier-town on the N; Ril, the former cap., 60 m. SSE from Cobbeh, on the route to Sennaar; Kubcabiyah, inhabited by Furians, Arabs, and Bergus; Kourma, a small town 12 m. W by S of Cobbeh, occupied by merchants, chiefly from Upper Egypt, called Jeiara; and Kurs, 6 hours NW by W of Cobbeh, inhabited by merchants, and a brutal set of people called Fucara. - Among the subordinate districts, which appear to be sometimes dependent on the Furian sultan, sometimes upon the neighbouring powers, are Dar Runga, which is generally subject to the sovereign of Bergu; Dar Berti or Bego, between Fur and Bergu; and Zoghawa, formerly an independent kingdom. Among the southern countries, whither the Jelabs of Fur and Bergu sometimes journey to procure slaves, is Dar Kulla, on the river Bahr Kulla.
DARGODO, a town of Russia, in Bessarabia, on the Kobolta river, 50 m. NNE of Jassy, on the road to Moghilev.

DARGO, or Dzarago, a village of Georgia, 40 m. E of the Dariel pass. It is an assemblage of mud huts, situate at the top of a deep defile. In 1845 it was the residence of Shamyl, the leader of the Caucasian tribes in their late struggle with Russia; and was taken by the latter, after a series of desperate conflicts, and with enormous loss. To reach it, the Russians had to storm in succession 26 palankas, or intrenchments formed of trunks of trees, crossing the valley over an extent of 20 m.
DARGUN, a bailiwick and town of Mecklenburg- Schwerin, in the circle of Wenden, 16 m. SE of Gnoyen, on an affluent of the Peene. Pop. of b. 5,000; of t. 800.
DAR-HUMMAR, a district of Kordofan, to the W of the central town of Oveid, on the route to Cobbeh.
DARIA. See SIR.

DARIEL, a pass of the Caucasus, in Georgia, leading from Kobi to the plain of the Kabarda, round the E side of the Kazbek. The base of the rocks on either side of this pass is washed by the Terek, and the road is excavated in the solid rock overhanging the foaming stream. Near the entrance of the pass are the ruins of an ancient fortress. See Caucasus.

DARIEN (GULF OF), the S portion of the Caribbean sea, on the N coast of New Grenada, between N lat. 7° 56' and 10°, and the meridians of 76° and 78° W. The Atrato or Darien has its delta at the head of the gulf, in what is usually called the bay of Candelaria. See Candelaria and Uraba.
DARIEN (ISTHMUS OF). See PANAMA.

DARIEN, a township in Fairfield co., in the state of Connecticut, U. S., 74 m. SW of Hartford. Pop. 1,080.—Also a township in Genesee co., in the state of New York, 263 m. W of Albany. Pop. 2,406.— Also the cap. of Mackintosh co., in Georgia, on the N side of the Altamaha, 12 m. above the bar of that river. - Also a township in Walworth co., in Wisconsin territory.

DARIEUGRAND, three peaks of the Pyrenees, in France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Aucun, 14 m. SSW of Argelès, at the distance of less than a mile from one another. Near them is a lake of the same name.

DARJEELING, a town in the principality of Sikkim, at the foot of the Himalaya mountains, in Northern India. The town stands upon a spur of the mountain Sinchul, at an alt. of 9,000 ft. Due N, at a distance of 60 m., the horizon is bounded by the great snowy range in which the peak of Kinchin- Junga attains an alt. of 28,172 ft., being the loftiest mountain at present known. Six or seven successive ranges of forest-clad mountains intervene between the town of D. and the snowy range; but, owing to the usual purity of the atmosphere, the distance appears to be only a day's journey .- Dr. J. D. Hooker.

DARKE, a county of the state of Ohio, U. S., comprising an area of 714 sq. m., drained by Stillwater and Greenville creeks. It is generally level, and very fertile. Pop. in 1830, 6,203; in 1840, 13,282. Its cap. is Greenville.

DARKEHMEN, a town of Prussia, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the prov. of East Prussia, regency and 15 m. SSW of Gumbinnen, and 65 m. ESE of Königsberg, on the Angerap. Pop. 2,223. It possesses manufactories of linen and cloth, and several tanneries and breweries. Corn forms the chief article of commerce.—The circle comprises 1 town, 7 parishes, and 21,105 inhabitants.
DARKESVILLE, a village of Berkley co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 162 m. N of Richmond, on Sulphur Springs creek, an affluent of the Potomac.

DARKHAN (MOUNT), a lofty mountain of Mongolia, in the Kalkha territory, in N lat. 47° 35'. It extends a considerable distance from N to S, and its lofty ridge is composed of steep rocks of red granite. From the summit there is a prospect over a boundless plain: towards the E are eight salt lakes, and to the W an immense stretch of country covered with pointed eminences.—Timkowski.
DAR-KUKA, a town of Central Africa, in the kingdom of Borgu or Dar-Saley, on the SE bank of lake Fittre, about 400 m. WSW of Cobbeh.

DAR-KULLA, a territory of Sudan, bounded on the N by the kingdom of Begharmi, on the NE by that of Borgu or Dar-Saley, on the E by the country of the Shiluks, and on the W by the kingdom of Bornu to which it is tributary and from which it is separated by the rivers Shary and Asu. Its southern limits are still unknown. The general character of the surface of this extensive region is described as low and swampy, abounding in streams and lakes, and to a great extent covered with luxuriant vegetation. The inhabitants are either black or coppercoloured. The slave-markets of Bornu and Dar-Fur are extensively supplied from this region.—Also a town in the kingdom of Borgu or Dar-Saley, 280 m. SSE of Dar-Kuka.

DARLASTON, a parish of Staffordshire, on the banks of the Trent, 1 m. NNW of Wednesbury, near the Grand Junction railway from Birmingham to Liverpool, and on the Easington canal. Area 770 acres. Pop. 8,244.—Also a township in the p. of Stone, in the same co. Pop. 222.
DARLEY, a parish of Derbyshire, 3 m. NW of Matlock, on the E bank of the Derwent. Area 7,860 acres. Pop. 1,929.
DARLEY-ABBEY, or LITTLE DERBY, a chapelry in the p. of Alkmund, Derbyshire, 14 m. N of Derby, on the Derwent, and on the Derby and Leeds railway. Pop. in 1801, 615; in 1841, 1,059.
DARLI, a town of Central Africa, in the kingdom of Borgu or Dar-Saley, 150 m. SSE of Dar-Kuka.

DARLING, a township of Upper Canada, in the Bathurst district, to the NW of the township of Lanark. The river Clyde runs through the S part of the township, and in the N is a large lake called Wabalac or White lake. Pop. in 1842, 271.

DARLING, a central river of Australia, which is formed by numerous streams descending from the W slope of the Coast range, between the parallels of 32° and 28° S. Of these the most northern is the Condamine, which, rising where the Coast range expands into the table-land, sweeps round from NE to SW, and, crossing the parallel of 28°, flows in a prevailingly SSW course to the meridian of 30°, a little below which it is supposed to be joined by the Peel river or Nammoy, the Castlereagh river, and the Macquarie, all coming from the SE. It then runs | mous revenue flows into Durham. Area of p. 7,630 DAR to the N of W, but again bends SW; and, a little to the W of the meridian of 46°, is joined by the Bogan nearly on the parallel of 30°, a river also coming from the SE, and whose course exceeds 300 m. From this point of confluence, the D. runs SW to about 32° 40' S lat., and 142° E long., where it turns S; and, pursuing, it is supposed, a course to the W of S for about 140 m., falls into the Murray, on the r. bank, in 141° 35' E long. The total length of its course probably exceeds 1,000 m.; and, in many places, it has sufficient depth for the purposes of inland navigation; but the country through which it flows is in great part a barren steppe, and the extent of alluvial soil on its banks appears to be very limited.

DARLING DOWNS, an extensive tract of elevated pastoral country on the NW skirts of New South Wales, in about the mean parallel of 28° S, having Peel's plains to the N, and Canning's plains on the S and SE. They have an elevation above sealevel of from 1,800 to 2,000 ft.; and ice is occasionally formed upon them in the calm clear nights of winter.

DARLING RANGE, a granitic range of hills running through nearly the whole length of the colony of Western Australia, or from about the parallel of 31° to Point D'Entrecasteaux, in a direction, generally speaking, N and S, at a distance of from 35 to 40 m. from the coast, and varying from 800 to 2,000 ft. in alt. above sea-level, gradually sinking towards the N. It forms, with few exceptions, a sterile belt: the surface consisting in great measure of stone of a red cellular structure, through which the granite has in some places protruded. The vegetation consists of coarse herbage, prickly flowering shrubs, heaths, and forests of mahogany and blue gum-trees. The land which now intervenes between this range and the shore appears to be a comparatively recent conquest from the sea. Shells still common on the adjacent coasts are met with 14 ft. below the surface, near the foot of the range. On the E side of the range, close to its base, are several groups of isolated conical hills, from a 1 m. to 1 m. apart, extending from the William river to the Tugee district, a distance of 120 m., and bearing strong evidences of ignition. The country further on to the eastward falls into sandy plains similar to those on the W side. Dr. Von Sumner has ascertained the presence of mercurial ores in some parts of this range; and of carboniferous deposits between the W coast and this range.
DARLINGS, a village of Butler township, Knox co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 73 m. NE of Columbus, on the S bank of Vernon river.
DARLINGSCOTT, a hamlet in the p. of Tredington, Worcestershire, locally in the co. of Warwick, 24 m. WNW of Shipston-upon-Stour. Pop. 176.

DARLINGTON, a parish and township in the co. and 18½ m. S of Durham. The town is situated on rising ground, on the W bank of the Skerne, 3 m. N of its junction with the Tees, and on the Great North of England railway, from York to Newcastle, by which it is 45 m. from York, and 38 m. from Newcastle. The Stockton and D. railway is continued from D. to Bishop-Auckland, where it unites with the Weardale and Frosterly railway. The town is built in the form of a square, whence several streets diverge in different directions. It is the place of election for the members for the S division of the co., and gives the title of Earl to the Marquis of Cleveland. D. forms the centre of an extensive coal-mining district. The amount annually received within 30 m. of D. for coals alone has been roughly estimated at £4,000,000. This area embraces Newcastle and its vicinity; but a large proportion of this enoracres. Pop. in 1801, 5,349; in 1841, 11,877. Area of township, inclusive of the hamlet of Axneyfield, 3,470 acres. Pop. 11,033.

DARLINGTON, a township of Upper Canada, in the Newcastle District, bounded on the S by Lake Ontario. It is well watered and generally fertile. Pop. in 1842, 3,500. On the lake is a port and village of the same name.

DARLINGTON, a district of South Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 1,050 sq. m., bounded on the NE by Great Pedee river, and by Lynch's creek on the SW. It has an undulating surface, and its soil on the rivers is extremely fertile. Pop. in 1830, 13,728; in 1840, 14,822. Its capital, of the same name, is situated on Black creek, an affluent of Great Pedee river, 129 m. ESE of Columbus. Pop. in 1840, 500.—Also a village of Little Beaver township, Beaver co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 239 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. 220.-Also a village of Harford co., in the state of Maryland, 21 m. W of the Susquehanna river, and 71 m. NE of Annapolis.
DARLTON, or DARLETON, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 31 m. ENE of Tuxford, and 2 m. W of the Trent. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 203.

DAR-MAHAS, or Mahass, a district of Nubia, to the N of Dongola and S of Sukkot, extending along ۰ both sides of the Nile to the distance of about 60 m. It contains numerous remains of antiquity. Its chief towns are Goorgote, Koke, and Haffir. This district is less populous than Sukkot.
DAR-MARRAH, a district of Central Africa, in the kingdom of Dar-Fur.

DARMOUT, a town of Nubia, in the district of Kenous or Barabras, 30 m. S of Es Suan, and 66 m. NE of Deir situated partly on an island, and partly on a steep rock on the r. bank of the Nile. In the vicinity are the ruins of a small town named Ebnietel-Kufera.

DARMSTADT, the capital of the grand-duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, and of the prov. of Starkenburg, in the bailiwick of the same name, in N lat. 49° 56' 24", E long. 8° 24' 49". It is situated on the river Darm, between the Maine and the Neckar, 8 m. E by N of the Rhine, and on the line of the Frankfort and Mannheim railway. It consists of an old and a new town. The former, enclosed within old massive walls, has a somewhat gloomy aspect; the new town is similarly defended, but is built in better style and has broad streets. There are 12 public squares and 72 streets within the walls. Among the public buildings are the ducal palace, with a library of 150,000 vols.; the military school; a museum of natural history; an excellent picture-gallery; an opera house; a palace for the heir-apparent; the arsenal, barracks, and magazines; a house-of-correction, and a spacious riding school 320 ft. long and 150 ft. broad, now a depot for artillery. Among the public institutions are a gymnasium with 12 professors; a pedagogium or seminary for teachers; an academy of design; and numerous elementary schools. There are a synagogue, a Lutheran, a Reformed, and a Roman Catholic church, within the walls. The latter is a handsome circular structure with a magnificent dome, supported by 28 large columns 50 ft. high. - D. is the residence of the grand duke of Hesse; and the seat of various public boards, and the civil and criminal courts. It contained a pop. of 15,450 in 1817, and 27,000 in 1846; and is chiefly dependent for its trade on the expenditure of the court, the public functionaries, and the garrison. There are a few woollen and linen manufactories, card and paper mills, and tanneries, in the town and its vicinity; but the whole town "is as still and tranquil as a village," so far as the bustle of trade or manufac- DAR tures is concerned. The environs of D., especially towards the mountains, are extremely picturesque; and the soil, which is sandy, is highly cultivated. In the vicinity is a curious magnetic rock.-D., under the emperor Louis of Bavaria, became a town of the principality of Katzenellenbogen. After the extinction of that family, it lost much of its importance, until George I., son of Philip the Great, made it his residence; since which period it has gradually increased in extent and consequence.
DARNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Le Dorat, 10 m. NNW of Bellac, on a height near the Vienne. Pop. 2,157. It has manufactories of pottery and wooden-ware.

DARNETAL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Rouen. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 17,550; in 1841, 18,334. The town is pleasantly situated in a narrow valley, on the Aubette, 2 m. E of Rouen. Pop. 5,982. It has extensive manufactories of cloth, flannel, varieties of cotton fabrics, spindles, &c., numerous cotton-spinning-mills, dye-works, print-fields, paper and fulling mills, &c. The trade consists in grain, cattle, and productions of local manufacture.

DARNEY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, arrond. of Mirecourt.-The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,797; in 1841, 12,532.—The town is on the r. bank of the Saône. Pop. 1,784. It has several tin-ware manufactories. Fairs are held six times a-year.
DARNHALL, a township in the p. of Whitgate, Cheshire, 5 m. WSW of Middlewich. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 197.
DARNHALL, a river in Radnorshire, an affluent of the Wye near Rhyader-Gowy.

DARNLEY'S ISLAND, an island of Australasia, in Torres strait, in S lat. 9° 39', E long. 142° 59'. It is about 15 m. in circumf., and presents a surface finely varied with hills and plains, and covered with rich vegetation. It is of volcanie origin, and attains an alt. of 500 ft. above the sea. It is inhabited by a race of tall savages, of a dark brown or chocolate colour, with frizzled hair, who ornament the interior of their conical bee-hive huts with human skulls and strings of hands. Their arms are bows and arrows, lances, and clubs; and they have canoes from 50 to 70 ft. in length. They call their island Erroob.
DAROCA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 20 m. SE of Calatayud, on the r. bank of the Xiloca. Pop. 2,863. It is a well-built town, and is ornamented with several beautiful fountains.
DARORE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, 55 m. E of Perinda.
DAROWA, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Pilsen, 4 m. SW of Radnitz, on the Mies. Pop. 178.
DAROWEN, a parish and village of Montgomeryshire, 4 m. ENE of Machynllaeth. Pop. 1,041.
DAROZEH, a town of Hindostan, in the Balaghaut ceded districts, 17 m. NW of Bellary. There is a magnificent tank here.
DARRINGTON, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SE of Pontefract. Area 4,590 acres. Pop. 668.
DARRSTOWN, a village in Butler co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 109 m. WSW of Columbus. Pop. 300.
DAR-RUNGA, a district of Sudan, in about 9° N lat., and between the Bahr el Abiad and Lake Chad.
DAR-SALEY. See Borgu.
DARSHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. ENE of Yoxford. Pop. 528.

DART, a river of Devonshire, formed by the union of the East and the West Dart both rising on Dartmoor, and flowing SE into the English channel, ~ into which it falls near Dartmouth, after a course of 40 m. The tide flows up to Totness, 10 m.

DARTFORD, a parish and market-town in Kent, 15 m. ESE of London, and 22 m. NW of Maidstone, on the great road from London to Canterbury and Dover, in a narrow valley on the river Darent, which is navigable for boats, and over which a bridge is here erected. Area of p. 4,150 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,406; in 1841, 5,619. The town consists of one wide street. The church is a spacious edifice. There are celebrated powder-mills in the vicinity of D.; and here the first mill for the preparation of iron in the manufacture of wire was erected. There are also paper and silk-mills in the vicinity. The first papermill was erected here in the reign of Elizabeth, on the present site of the powder-mills, by Sir John Spilman, a German, who introduced the manufacture of paper into this kingdom. A ship canal has been projected to connect D. and Crayford with the Thames, capable of admitting steam and other vessels of 400 tons burden at all times of the tide; and thus, in effect, to convert D. and Crayford into seaport towns.
DARTINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. N by W of Totness. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 603.

DARTMOOR, an extensive and remarkable tract of high land on the NW of Exmoor, in the p. of Lidford, co. of Devon, 10 m. NE of Tavistock. It comprises an area of 350,000 acres, about one-seventh part of which is termed Dartmoor Forest, and belongs to the duchy of Cornwall. Its mean height above sea-level is about 1,200 ft. This elevated tract is of an irregular form, broken into numerous minor hills, many of them crowned by picturesque groups of granite rocks, provincially termed tors, and for the most part consisting of a wild mixture of heath, bog, rock, and rapid streams, whence some of the principal, and many of the smaller, rivers of Devon take their rise. De La Beche states, that the Yes tor, apparently the most elevated point in this high land, rises 2,050 ft. above the sea. There are several other mountains nearly as elevated. The summit of Cawsand beacon, on the N of Dartmoor, one of the stations of the great triangulation on which the ordnance maps are based, is 1,792 ft. above the sea; but several others are higher. D. is the principal metalliferous district of Devon. Viewed as a whole, it is of a mixed metalliferous character, and affords large quantities of most of the useful metals, with valuable mines of manganese. The soil is mostly of a deep black colour, consisting of peat-bog, varying in depth from 2 to 20 ft. During the revolutionary war, a French prison was erected on the moor, which has been transformed into an agricultural settlement for the poor. Part of the buildings are now occupied by a company established to extract naphtha from the peat. The prisons were commenced in 1806; they are built of the granite found on the moor, and cost about £127,000. Two of the prisons, a row of houses for subordinate officers, and the chapel-walls, were erected by French, whilst the interior of the chapel was fitted by American prisoners, who received from government a small gratuity for their labour. At one period of the war as many as 10,000 prisoners were confined within the walls. The site of the prisons-which comprise a circular admeasurement of 30 acres-is about 1,400 ft. above the sea. The effect of this upon the health of the prisoners may be estimated from the following extract from a return: "From May 29, 1809, to April 22, 1814, as many as 12,679 prisoners underwent confinement, of whom 1,117 died, consisting of 1,095 French and 22 Americans." The mortality in the prison of D. was, therefore, much less than any town-average. Prince Town, the new establishment, the prov. of Bijapur. A portion of D. is now under promising cultivation.

DARTMOUTH, a borough, seaport, and markettown in Devonshire, 31 m. SSW of Exeter, 202 m. WSW of London, and 8 m. distant from the Great Western railway at Totness. Area of b. 1,650 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,398; in 1841, 4,663. The town is situated at the mouth of the river Dart, on its W bank, on the declivity of an eminence sloping gradually to the margin of the water; and the houses are ranged in streets rising in a terraced form at different elevations. The harbour, the entrance to which is defended by a castle and two batteries, is capacious enough to receive 500 sail of vessels, and is remarkable for its security, though rather narrow of entrance, and for not having a bar. The light-tower in the castle on the W side of the entrance, is in lat. 50° 21' N, long. 3° 33' W. The quays project irregularly into the river, and cause an apparent curvature in its course. A floating bridge is here impelled across the river upon chains by steam, and is of great size and accommodation, being employed on a river of about 1,650 ft. in breadth, and of great depth and rapidity. There is daily communication with Totness by means of a steamer on the Dart.-D. sent two members to parliament up to the passing of the reform act, when it was deprived of one. The number of electors in 1847 was 282.—The trade of D. arose in a great measure from the pilchard and Newfoundland fisheries, which of late have considerably declined. The chief markets are the Mediterranean ports, the returns from which are generally wine, oil, salt, and fruit, &c. The chief exports at present are cider and barley. The coasting trade is also extensive. The gross receipt of customs in 1838 was £4,100 13s. 2d.; in 1846, £4,075. There are here bonded warehouses for all goods except tobacco and East India goods. The shipping belonging to this port in 1848 consisted of 452 vessels = 32,099 tons.—In the parliamentary war, D. being considered a very important post, was warmly contested by both parties, and surrendered in 1643, after a siege of 4 weeks, to Prince Maurice, who garrisoned it for the king; but in 1646 it was retaken by General Fairfax. D. gives the title of Earl to the Legge family.

DARTMOUTH, a flourishing township of Nova Scotia, in the co. and district of Halifax, on the E side of the harbour of that name, which is here about a mile in width. It is intersected by a chain of lakes, which, with the Shubenacadie canal, forms a communication with the basin of Minas. The town of D. is situated on the harbour, opposite Halifax.—Also a township of Bristol co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 65 m. S of Boston, intersected by Aponiganset river, which flows into Buzzard's bay, in the SE of the co. Pop. in 1840, 4,135.

DARTMOUTH, or ONGLAHE, a river of Madagascar, in the Buques territory, which descends from the W side of the Botismene mountains; runs W; and after a course of 165 m., throws itself into the bay of St. Augustin, under the tropic of Cancer.
DARTON, a parish and township of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. NW of Barnsley, on the Dearne. Area of p. 4,510 acres. Pop. 3,583. Area of township 1,400 acres. Pop. 1,692.
DARTUCH (CAPE), a low flat cape forming the SW point of the island of Minorca, in N lat. 39° 55', E long. 3° 51'.
DARUVAR, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 32 m. NW of Posega. Pop. 470. There are mineral springs here of considerable repute. Black marble, veined with red, is quarried in the vicinity.

DARWAR, a district and town of Hindostan, in 28'. It has a strong fort. In 1784 it was wrested from the Mahrattas by Tippu; but in 1791 was retaken by the assistance of three battalions of Bombay sepoys, after a siege of 29 weeks' duration. The surrounding territory is sometimes known as the Southern Mahratta country. The W part of this territory has a wet climate, and is in great part covered with forests: rice is cultivated in this quarter. The middle, E, and SE districts are better suited for dry crops. There are extensive tracts of regur, or cotton ground, in which the soilformed apparently by the disintegration of trap rocks -is from 2 to 30 ft. deep, and is as remarkable for its fertility as for its great extent. Cotton, juari, wheat, and other grains, are raised from it in succession, without the application of any manure. A candy or 500 lbs. of clean cotton can generally be bought at D. for 62 rupees. The pop. of this territory when it came into possession of the English in 1818 was 600,000.
DARWEN (Lower), a township in the p. and 3 m. SE of Blackburn, Lancashire, near the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 2,490 acres. Pop. 3,707.

DARWEN (OVER), a chapelry in the p. and 4 m. SSE of Blackburn, Lancashire. Area 5,010 acres. Pop. 9,348. Extensive cotton-mills and paper-works afford employment to a large proportion of the pop. of this and the contiguous township of Lower D.
DARWIN, a village in Clark co., in the state of Illinois, U.S., 133 m. SE by E of Springfield, on the Wabash river.
DARWIN (Mount), a mountain on the S coast of Eastern Tierra-del-Fuego, in S lat. 54° 45', W long. 69° 20'. Alt. 6,800 ft.

DARWIN (PORT), an inlet on the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 12° 27′ 45″, long. 1° 19′ 40″ E of Port Essington. The tide is very irregular here: rising at springs 24 ft., and at neaps sometimes only 2 ft.
DARWIN SOUND, a channel in the Tierra-del- Fuego archipelago, between Whale-boat sound on the W, and North-west arm on the E; intersected by the parallel of 54° 48' S lat.
DASAN, an islet in the Philippine group, SW of Mindanao, in N lat. 6° 48'.
DASBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle and 18 m. SSW of Prum, on the l. bank of the Our. Pop. 600.
DASCHKOFKA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Mohilev, 14 m. N of Bychof, on the r. bank of the Dnieper.
DASCHITZ, or Dassice, a village of Bohemia, in the circle and 8 m. NNE of Chrudim, on the Lanczabach. Pop. 1,492.

DASHKESEN, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, near the E side of Lake Urumiyah, 6 m. S of Dekergan. It is inhabited by the labourers employed in very extensive marble pits which lie a few hundred yards to the r. of the road, among the prongs of the Sehend range. These pits extend over a space about a 1 m. in circumf. Their sides are cut perpendicularly to the depth of 10 or 12 ft.; and in the section thus exposed the strata of marble is seen running in parallel and horizontal layers, of an average thickness of 7 or 8 inches. It is semi-crystalline, and when formed into thin plates is nearly transparent.
DASHOUR, a village of Lower Egypt, 20 m. S of Ghizeh, on the l. bank of the canal of the Nile, in the neighbourhood of the pyramids.
